Category: --- TRADES: the labor crafts and specialties that build a construction project. 25 trade families covering every distinct craft on vertical construction. ---

Description: General construction labor across all project phases — site prep through closeout. Lifecycle: mobilize → work → demobilize. Sub-specialties include site laborers (earthwork, erosion control, flagging), interior laborers (cleanup, protection, material movement during finishes), and demolition workers (selective/structural demo, hazmat abatement). Typically the largest headcount on early-phase daily logs. Decision authority: superintendent.
Industry Standard: OSHA 29 CFR 1926; NCCER Core Curriculum
Common Values: Site Laborer, Interior Laborer, Demolition Worker, Flagging, Material Handler
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; Foundation Software; HCSS HeavyJob
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily log manpower captures crew counts and hours; vendor directory tracks subcontractors; workforce timecard tables exist but not in gold layer
Benchmarking Use: Compare crew size and labor hours per $1M project value across project types and phases
Notes: Category-level entry. Sub-specialties rarely distinguished in data — usually reported as one headcount. Demolition workers may require separate licensing (hazmat, asbestos).

Description: Rough and finish carpentry — one of the most versatile trades on any project. Rough carpenters build formwork, shoring, blocking, backing, and temporary structures during foundations and structural phases. Finish carpenters install doors, frames, hardware, trim, and casework during interiors. Millworkers handle custom paneling and specialty wood on high-end projects. Often two different subcontractors for rough vs. finish work.
Industry Standard: CSI MasterFormat 06 00 00; NCCER Carpentry
Common Values: Rough Carpenter, Finish Carpenter, Millworker, Form Carpenter
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; Foundation Software
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily log manpower tracks crew counts; cost codes under Division 06; no standardized trade taxonomy distinguishes rough from finish
Benchmarking Use: Compare carpentry labor hours per SF by project type and building system
Notes: One of the most common trades on daily logs. Rough and finish are often different subs but may share a cost code.

Description: All concrete forming, placing, and finishing — from foundations through elevated slabs. Sub-specialties: concrete finishers (screeding, finishing, curing flatwork), formwork carpenters (construction and stripping), and post-tension specialists (strand placement, stressing, grouting on PT structures). Production measured in CY placed per day. Pour records in daily logs are a strong signal.
Industry Standard: CSI MasterFormat 03 00 00; ACI 301; ACI 318
Common Values: Concrete Finisher, Formwork Carpenter, Post-Tension Specialist
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; HCSS HeavyJob; Tekla
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily log manpower and productivity logs capture pour records; cost codes under Division 03
Benchmarking Use: Compare CY placed per crew-hour and pour cycle time by structural system type
Notes: Overlaps with carpentry (formwork). Post-tension is a distinct specialty sub — present only on PT structures.

Description: Steel erection and reinforcing — structural steel columns, beams, and deck plus reinforcing bar placement. Sub-specialties: structural steel erectors (bolting, plumbing, decking), welders (certified structural and misc welding), rebar installers (tying, splicing, cage assembly), and riggers/signal persons (crane rigging, heavy lifts). Production measured in tons erected per day. Critical path trade during structure phase.
Industry Standard: CSI MasterFormat 05 00 00; AISC 360; AWS D1.1; OSHA Steel Erection 1926 Subpart R
Common Values: Structural Steel Erector, Welder, Rebar Installer, Rigger / Signal Person
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; Tekla; SDS/2; Fabsuite
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily log manpower captures crew presence; cost codes under Division 05; RFIs for connection details are a strong structural complexity signal
Benchmarking Use: Compare steel erection rate (tons/day) and structural RFI density per ton across project types
Notes: Riggers/signal persons required for all crane operations. Welder may be under ironworker crew or a separate specialty. Rebar sometimes called 'rodbusting' — may be a separate sub.

Description: All unit masonry — brick, block, and stone. Sub-specialties: bricklayers (face brick, thin brick veneer), block layers (CMU walls, backup walls, elevator shafts), and stone masons (natural and manufactured stone for exterior veneer and interior lobbies). Weather-sensitive — production depends on temperature and moisture. Measured in SF of wall face per day.
Industry Standard: CSI MasterFormat 04 00 00; TMS 402/602; BIA Technical Notes
Common Values: Bricklayer, Block Layer, Stone Mason
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; Foundation Software
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily log manpower captures crew counts; cost codes under Division 04; daily log weather conditions affect production
Benchmarking Use: Compare masonry production rate (SF/day) and waste rate by wall type and weather zone
Notes: Masonry is seasonal in cold climates — hot weather mortar and cold weather protection add cost. Stone is often a separate specialty sub from brick/block.

Description: All roofing work — membrane, metal, and built-up systems. Sub-specialties: membrane roofers (TPO, PVC, EPDM, BUR, modified bitumen) and metal roofers/sheet metal workers (standing seam, metal panels, copings, flashing). Highly weather-sensitive — daily log delays frequently reference roofing holds. Manufacturer warranty submittals are a key deliverable. Critical path on most projects.
Industry Standard: CSI MasterFormat 07 50 00 – 07 60 00; NRCA Roofing Manual; FM Global
Common Values: Membrane Roofer, Metal Roofer / Sheet Metal, Built-Up Roofer
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; Foundation Software
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily log manpower plus delay records capture weather holds; cost codes under Division 07
Benchmarking Use: Compare roofing installation rate (squares/day) and weather delay frequency by climate zone
Notes: Metal roofer overlaps with sheet metal worker. Weather sensitivity means daily log delay records are especially useful for roofing benchmarks.

Description: All glass and curtain wall work — unitized and stick-built curtain wall systems, punch windows, storefronts, entrances, and skylights. Highly specialized — usually one sub for the full building envelope scope. Unitized systems are fabricated off-site and installed as complete units. Long lead time for fabrication. Often critical path on high-rise projects.
Industry Standard: CSI MasterFormat 08 40 00 – 08 80 00; AAMA; ASTM E2190
Common Values: Curtain Wall Installer, Window / Storefront Installer, Skylight Installer
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; Bluebeam; Revit
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily log manpower tracks presence; submittals capture shop drawings and performance specs; cost codes under Division 08
Benchmarking Use: Compare curtain wall installation rate (panels/day) and submittal cycle time by system type
Notes: Often the same crew for the full envelope. Submittal turnaround is a leading indicator of schedule risk for glazing.

Description: Below-grade and above-grade moisture protection — foundation membranes, air/vapor barriers, and joint sealants. Sub-specialties: below-grade waterproofers (fluid-applied, sheet membrane, bentonite), air/vapor barrier installers (building wrap, fluid-applied, self-adhered), and caulkers/sealant applicators (joint sealants, expansion joints, firestopping). Failure leads to long-term moisture intrusion — a top rework driver.
Industry Standard: CSI MasterFormat 07 10 00 – 07 92 00; ASTM E2112; ABAA
Common Values: Below-Grade Waterproofer, Air Barrier Installer, Caulker / Sealant Applicator
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; Bluebeam
Procore Coverage: Medium — quality observations capture waterproofing failures; cost codes under Division 07
Benchmarking Use: Compare waterproofing defect rate and rework cost as % of envelope budget
Notes: Waterproofing failures are among the top rework drivers — observation data is a strong signal. Air barrier is increasingly a code requirement (IECC).

Description: Metal framing and gypsum board — one of the largest interior workforces. Sub-specialties: metal framers (light-gauge steel framing, furring, soffits), drywall hangers (board hanging, specialty boards for moisture/abuse/fire), tapers/finishers (joint taping, mudding, sanding), and plasterers (traditional plaster, EIFS, stucco). Large crews during interior rough-in phase.
Industry Standard: CSI MasterFormat 09 20 00 – 09 29 00; ASTM C840; GA-216
Common Values: Metal Framer, Drywall Hanger, Taper / Finisher, Plasterer
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; Foundation Software
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily log manpower captures large crew presence; cost codes under Division 09
Benchmarking Use: Compare drywall installation rate (SF/day) and crew size per floor by project type
Notes: Plasterer is specialty — not on every project. Metal framing and hanging are often the same sub but different crews. EIFS crosses into exterior work.

Description: All painting and coating work — interior and exterior. Sub-specialties: interior painters (walls, ceilings, trim, doors), exterior painters (elastomeric, high-performance coatings), and specialty coatings applicators (epoxy floors, intumescent fireproofing, anti-graffiti). One of the last trades during finishes. Punch items frequently reference paint touch-ups.
Industry Standard: CSI MasterFormat 09 90 00 – 09 97 00; MPI Standards; SSPC
Common Values: Interior Painter, Exterior Painter, Specialty Coatings Applicator
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; Foundation Software
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily log manpower captures crew counts; punch items reference paint defects at high rates
Benchmarking Use: Compare paint crew size and punch item rate per 1000 SF by project type
Notes: Paint/finish is consistently among the top punch item categories across all project types. Exterior painting is weather-sensitive.

Description: All flooring installation — tile, carpet, resilient, hardwood, and specialty. Sub-specialties: tile setters (ceramic, porcelain, natural stone), carpet/resilient installers (carpet, LVT, VCT, sheet vinyl, rubber), and hardwood/specialty floor installers (hardwood, terrazzo, polished concrete, resinous). Often multiple flooring subs per project. Late-phase — substrate moisture is a common delay driver.
Industry Standard: CSI MasterFormat 09 30 00 – 09 68 00; TCNA Handbook; CRI; NWFA
Common Values: Tile Setter, Carpet / Resilient Installer, Hardwood / Specialty Floor Installer
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; Foundation Software
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily log manpower tracks crews; submittals capture product specs; punch items reference flooring defects
Benchmarking Use: Compare flooring defect rate by material type and installer
Notes: Moisture testing delays are common — concrete moisture content must meet ASTM F2170 before resilient flooring installation. Different sub for each flooring type on many projects.

Description: Ceiling systems — acoustic grid-and-tile and specialty ceilings. Sub-specialties: acoustic ceiling installers (grid, lay-in tile, tegular) and specialty ceiling installers (wood, metal, stretch, linear, cloud ceilings). Sometimes combined with drywall sub. Follows MEP rough-in above ceiling.
Industry Standard: CSI MasterFormat 09 50 00 – 09 54 00; ASTM E1264; CISCA
Common Values: Acoustic Ceiling Installer, Specialty Ceiling Installer
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; Foundation Software
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily log manpower captures crews; cost codes under Division 09
Benchmarking Use: Compare ceiling installation rate (SF/day) and coordination delay frequency
Notes: Ceiling grid installation cannot begin until above-ceiling MEP is inspected and approved. Coordination delays are common.

Description: Thermal and acoustic insulation — building envelope and mechanical systems. Sub-specialties: building insulation (batt, rigid, spray foam, blown-in for walls and roof) and mechanical insulation (pipe and duct insulation, jacketing). Mechanical insulation is a distinct specialty — often under the HVAC sub's scope.
Industry Standard: CSI MasterFormat 07 21 00; IECC; ASHRAE 90.1; NIA
Common Values: Building Insulation Installer, Mechanical Insulation Installer
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; Foundation Software
Procore Coverage: Low — rarely its own cost code in daily logs; often bundled with drywall or HVAC
Benchmarking Use: Compare insulation cost per SF of envelope by climate zone and building type
Notes: Building insulation increasingly drives energy code compliance. Mechanical insulation is a separate sub on larger projects.

Description: Fire protection of structural elements and through-penetrations. Sub-specialties: spray fireproofers (SFRM on structural steel — follows steel erection, critical path) and firestoppers (through-penetration and perimeter fire barrier — inspection-critical, MEP coordination-heavy). Required for fire-rated assemblies.
Industry Standard: CSI MasterFormat 07 81 00 – 07 84 00; UL Firestop Systems; IBC Chapter 7
Common Values: Spray Fireproofer (SFRM), Firestopper
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Medium — inspections and observations capture firestopping deficiencies; cost codes under Division 07
Benchmarking Use: Compare firestopping inspection pass rate and spray fireproofing cost per SF of steel
Notes: Spray fireproofing follows steel erection — critical path item. Firestopping deficiencies are among the most common inspection findings.

Description: All plumbing and piping — domestic water, waste, vent, gas, and specialty process piping. Sub-specialties: plumbers (domestic water and DWV), steamfitters/HVAC pipefitters (chilled water, hot water, steam, refrigerant), medical gas installers (healthcare projects — separate certification), and process pipefitters (lab, industrial, clean room). On site from underground rough-in through fixture trim.
Industry Standard: CSI MasterFormat 22 00 00; IPC; UPC; ASME B31
Common Values: Plumber, Steamfitter / HVAC Pipefitter, Medical Gas Installer, Process Pipefitter
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; Foundation Software
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily log manpower tracks crews; cost codes under Division 22; inspections capture pressure test results
Benchmarking Use: Compare plumbing labor hours per fixture count by building type
Notes: Steamfitter may be under HVAC sub's scope — union distinction. Medical gas requires separate certification and testing.

Description: All HVAC ductwork and equipment — fabrication, installation, and commissioning. Sub-specialties: sheet metal workers (duct fabrication and installation), HVAC equipment installers (AHUs, RTUs, chillers, boilers, VRF), test and balance technicians (air and water balancing — required for closeout), and controls technicians (BAS/BMS programming, sensor installation). Often the largest MEP sub by contract value.
Industry Standard: CSI MasterFormat 23 00 00; ASHRAE Standards; SMACNA; AABC/NEBB
Common Values: Sheet Metal Worker, HVAC Equipment Installer, TAB Technician, Controls Technician
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; Foundation Software; Tridium (controls)
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily log manpower tracks crews; cost codes under Division 23; commissioning inspections track system startup
Benchmarking Use: Compare HVAC labor hours per ton of cooling by building type and system type
Notes: Controls technician may be under HVAC sub or a separate controls sub. TAB and commissioning are late-project activities required for closeout.

Description: All electrical work — power distribution, lighting, fire alarm, and site electrical. Sub-specialties: power electricians (service entrance, distribution, branch circuits, devices), lighting electricians (fixture installation, controls, emergency), fire alarm electricians (FA devices, wiring, panels, programming), and site electricians (underground ductbank, site lighting, utility connections). On site from underground through punch.
Industry Standard: CSI MasterFormat 26 00 00; NEC (NFPA 70); NFPA 72; OSHA Electrical
Common Values: Power Electrician, Lighting Electrician, Fire Alarm Electrician, Site Electrician
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; Foundation Software
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily log manpower tracks crews; cost codes under Division 26; inspections capture code compliance
Benchmarking Use: Compare electrical labor hours per SF by building type and occupancy classification
Notes: Fire alarm work may be under electrical sub or a separate FA specialty sub — varies by market. Electrical is typically on site the longest of any MEP trade.

Description: All fire suppression piping — wet, dry, pre-action, deluge, and specialty systems. Sub-specialties: wet system installers (mains, branches, heads for standard wet pipe) and specialty system installers (dry, pre-action, deluge, clean agent, kitchen hood). Separate union and licensing from plumbers. Coordinates closely with structure (hangers) and ceilings (head locations).
Industry Standard: CSI MasterFormat 21 00 00; NFPA 13; NFPA 13R; FM Global
Common Values: Wet System Installer, Specialty System Installer
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; Foundation Software
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily log manpower tracks crews; cost codes under Division 21; inspections capture hydrostatic test results
Benchmarking Use: Compare sprinkler head count per SF and installation rate by occupancy type
Notes: Separate union and licensing from plumbing. Typically one sprinkler sub per project. NFPA 13 compliance is inspection-critical.

Description: All elevator and vertical transportation work — traction, hydraulic, MRL, escalators, and moving walks. Typically proprietary — one manufacturer's installer per project. Long lead time for equipment. Temporary construction elevator (hoist) is a separate scope from permanent elevators.
Industry Standard: CSI MasterFormat 14 00 00; ASME A17.1; ANSI A17.1
Common Values: Traction Elevator, Hydraulic Elevator, MRL Elevator, Escalator
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily log manpower tracks presence; cost codes under Division 14; vendor directory identifies elevator sub
Benchmarking Use: Compare elevator installation duration per floor and cost per stop by system type
Notes: Proprietary trade — usually one sub per project. Temporary construction hoist is a distinct scope (see Equipment). Elevator inspection is jurisdictional.

Description: All low voltage and technology systems — data/telecom, security, AV, and fire alarm. Sub-specialties: data/telecom installers (structured cabling, fiber, racks), security installers (cameras, access control, intrusion), AV installers (audio, video, conferencing), and fire alarm installers (system design, installation, programming). Often multiple specialty subs per project.
Industry Standard: CSI MasterFormat 27 00 00 – 28 00 00; BICSI; NFPA 72; TIA/EIA-568
Common Values: Data / Telecom Installer, Security Installer, AV Installer, Fire Alarm Installer
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; IT integrators
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily log manpower tracks crews; cost codes under Divisions 27-28; submittals capture system specs
Benchmarking Use: Compare low voltage cost per SF by building type and technology density
Notes: Fire alarm installer overlaps with electrician — licensing varies by jurisdiction. AV is increasingly complex and a separate specialty sub.

Description: Operation of construction equipment — earthmoving, cranes, and material handling. Sub-specialties: heavy equipment operators (excavators, dozers, loaders, graders, scrapers), crane operators (tower and mobile cranes — separately licensed), and forklift/telehandler operators. Crane operators require specific NCCCO certification. Equipment production rates drive earthwork and structural schedules.
Industry Standard: OSHA 1926 Subpart N/O/CC; NCCCO Certification
Common Values: Heavy Equipment Operator, Crane Operator, Forklift / Telehandler Operator
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; HCSS HeavyJob; fleet telematics
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily log equipment captures utilization; daily log manpower captures operator headcount
Benchmarking Use: Compare equipment hours per CY of earthwork by soil condition and project type
Notes: Crane operators have separate licensing (NCCCO). Forklift/telehandler certification (OSHA) required for all operators.

Description: Survey and layout — establishing control points, building layout, grade verification, and as-built documentation. Uses total stations, GPS/GNSS, robotic instruments, and increasingly drones for progress documentation. First on site and last to leave. Layout accuracy directly affects every subsequent trade.
Industry Standard: CSI MasterFormat 01 71 23; NSPS Standards
Common Values: Land Surveyor, Layout Technician, Drone/UAV Operator
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; Trimble; Leica; DJI; Pix4D
Procore Coverage: Low — daily log manpower may capture survey crew; no structured survey data in most PM systems
Benchmarking Use: Compare survey cost as % of project value and layout error rate by building complexity
Notes: Drone/UAV surveys are increasingly common for progress photos, volumetric surveys, and thermal scans. Survey data lives outside PM systems.

Description: Safety management and compliance — hazard identification, incident investigation, OSHA compliance, and safety training. Typically a full-time role on projects above a threshold size. Generates safety observations, manages toolbox talks, and oversees PPE compliance. Decision authority on stop-work conditions.
Industry Standard: OSHA 29 CFR 1926; ANSI Z10; ISO 45001
Common Values: Site Safety Manager, Safety Engineer, Safety Officer
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; iAuditor; Safety platforms
Procore Coverage: High — safety observations, daily log safety violations, and project role memberships provide structured tracking
Benchmarking Use: Compare recordable incident rate (TRIR) and observation frequency per 200K hours by project type
Notes: Project role memberships identify safety professionals. Observation category = Safety is the primary data signal. Not all projects have a dedicated safety role.

Description: Quality assurance and testing — inspection of installed work, material testing coordination, and punch list management. May be a dedicated role or part of the superintendent's responsibilities. Generates quality observations and manages inspection checklists. Third-party testing (concrete, soil, fireproofing) results are tracked externally.
Industry Standard: ASTM testing standards; IBC Chapter 17; ACI 318 Ch 26
Common Values: QC Manager, QC Inspector, Third-Party Testing Coordinator
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; PlanGrid; Fieldwire; iAuditor
Procore Coverage: High — inspections, quality observations, and punch items provide structured quality data
Benchmarking Use: Compare inspection deficiency rate and first-pass inspection success rate by trade and system
Notes: Quality observations and inspection items are the strongest signals. Third-party test results typically live outside PM systems.

Description: Building systems commissioning — functional performance testing of MEP systems before owner occupancy. Increasingly required by code (IECC, ASHRAE 189.1) and LEED. Independent from installing contractors. Tests integrated system performance, not just individual equipment. Lifecycle: pre-functional checks → startup → TAB → integrated testing → performance verification.
Industry Standard: ASHRAE Guideline 0; ASHRAE 202; ACG Commissioning Guideline
Common Values: Commissioning Authority (CxA), Retro-Commissioning Agent
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; Cx platforms (BxP, FCA)
Procore Coverage: Low — project role memberships may identify Cx agent; no structured commissioning data in most PM systems
Benchmarking Use: Compare commissioning duration and deficiency count per system across building types
Notes: Distinct from TAB technician (HVAC-specific). Cx covers all MEP systems and building envelope. Growing requirement — increasingly a code mandate.

Category: --- EQUIPMENT: construction equipment mobilized to a project site. 12 categories covering lifting, earthmoving, concrete, access, hauling, and temporary services. ---

Description: Lifting equipment for vertical construction — tower cranes, mobile cranes, and overhead gantry systems. Tower cranes: hammerhead (fixed jib, high-rise), luffing (variable radius, tight urban sites), and self-erecting (low/mid-rise). Mobile cranes: truck-mounted (steel picks, equipment setting), crawler (heavy lifts, piling), and all-terrain. Crane erection and dismantling are major schedule milestones. Crane capacity and reach govern structural sequencing.
Industry Standard: OSHA 1926 Subpart CC; ASME B30; NCCCO Certification
Common Values: Tower Crane — Hammerhead, Tower Crane — Luffing, Tower Crane — Self-Erecting, Mobile Crane — Truck, Mobile Crane — Crawler, Mobile Crane — All-Terrain, Overhead / Gantry Crane
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; HCSS; Crane rental: ALL, Maxim, Bigge; Fleet telematics
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily log equipment captures crane presence and utilization; no structured lift planning data
Benchmarking Use: Compare crane months per project and crane cost as % of structural budget by building height
Notes: Crane erection/dismantling noted in daily logs. Tower crane selection drives structural pour sequence. Multiple cranes on large sites require coordination.

Description: Excavation, grading, and site preparation equipment. Types: track excavators (primary dig — bulk and detail), mini excavators (confined spaces, utilities), long-reach excavators (deep cuts), wheel loaders (material handling, stockpile), skid steers (compact, versatile with attachments), backhoes (combined loader/excavator for utilities), dozers (grading, pushing), graders (fine grading, road building), and trenchers (narrow utility trenching). GPS machine guidance increasingly standard.
Industry Standard: OSHA 1926 Subpart O; Caterpillar/Komatsu/Deere specs
Common Values: Track Excavator, Mini Excavator, Long Reach, Wheel Loader, Skid Steer, Backhoe, Dozer, Grader, Trencher
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; HCSS HeavyJob; Fleet: Caterpillar VisionLink, John Deere JDLink, Komatsu KOMTRAX
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily log equipment captures equipment on site; no structured production rate data
Benchmarking Use: Compare earthwork production rate (CY/day) and equipment cost per CY by soil condition and project size
Notes: GPS machine guidance reduces survey staking. Skid steers are the most versatile — used from earthwork through interior cleanup. Telematics provide utilization data but rarely integrated into PM systems.

Description: Concrete placement and finishing equipment — pumps, mixers, vibrators, and saws. Boom pumps (truck-mounted, for elevated pours), line pumps (ground-level, for slabs and foundations), mixer trucks (ready-mix delivery), concrete vibrators (internal consolidation), and concrete saws (flat, wall, and wire saws for cutting and demo). Pump selection depends on placement distance and elevation. Pour volume per day drives equipment sizing.
Industry Standard: ACI 304R; ASTM C94
Common Values: Boom Pump, Line Pump, Mixer Truck, Concrete Vibrator, Concrete Saw
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; Concrete suppliers; Fleet telematics
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily log equipment and daily log productivity capture pour records; no structured pump data
Benchmarking Use: Compare concrete placement rate (CY/hour) and pump cost per CY by pour type
Notes: Boom pump vs line pump selection depends on reach and access. Concrete saw is also used for demo and modification work. Mixer truck count per pour is a productivity signal.

Description: Equipment for moving materials on site — forklifts, telehandlers, conveyors, and hoists. Rough terrain forklifts (outdoor material handling), warehouse forklifts (indoor staging), telehandlers (variable-reach telescopic handler — most common material handling on site), and conveyors/hoists (material conveyors, trash chutes). Telehandlers are on every project. Material handling efficiency directly affects trade productivity.
Industry Standard: OSHA 1926 Subpart N; OSHA Powered Industrial Trucks
Common Values: Forklift — Rough Terrain, Forklift — Warehouse, Telehandler, Conveyor / Hoist
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; Rental companies; Fleet telematics
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily log equipment captures presence; no structured utilization tracking
Benchmarking Use: Compare material handling equipment hours per $1M project value by project type
Notes: Telehandler is the workhorse — present on virtually every project. Forklift certification (OSHA) required for all operators. Equipment rental vs ownership is a key cost decision.

Description: Equipment for elevated work access — boom lifts, scissor lifts, personnel hoists, and scaffolding. Boom lifts: articulating (around obstacles) and telescopic (maximum reach). Scissor lifts: vertical platform for interior/exterior work — most common access during MEP rough-in. Personnel/material hoists: temporary construction elevators — schedule-critical on high-rise. Scaffolding: tubular frame, modular system, mast climbing, and swing stages for facade work.
Industry Standard: OSHA 1926 Subpart L (Scaffolds); ANSI A92 (Aerial Lifts)
Common Values: Boom Lift — Articulating, Boom Lift — Telescopic, Scissor Lift, Personnel Hoist, Scaffolding — Tubular, Scaffolding — System, Mast Climbing, Swing Stage
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; Rental: United, Sunbelt, BrandSafway
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily log equipment captures access equipment on site; no structured utilization data
Benchmarking Use: Compare access equipment rental cost as % of total project cost by building height and type
Notes: Hoist availability directly affects productivity on high-rise — hoists are schedule-critical. Mast climbing platforms are replacing traditional scaffold on many projects. Scaffolding requires competent person inspections.

Description: Soil and base compaction equipment — rollers, plate compactors, and rammers. Vibratory rollers (smooth drum or padfoot for large areas), plate compactors (walk-behind for trench backfill and confined areas), and pneumatic rollers (multi-tire for asphalt). Compaction equipment selection depends on soil type and lift thickness. Proof rolling and compaction testing are quality checkpoints.
Industry Standard: ASTM D698; ASTM D1557; AASHTO T99/T180
Common Values: Vibratory Roller, Plate Compactor, Pneumatic Roller, Rammer / Jumping Jack
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; HCSS; Geotechnical testing firms
Procore Coverage: Low — daily log equipment may capture presence; compaction test results live in geotechnical reports
Benchmarking Use: Compare compaction pass rates and recompaction frequency by soil type
Notes: Third-party geotechnical testing results (density tests) are the real quality signal but live outside PM systems. Compaction failures cause schedule delays.

Description: Deep foundation equipment — pile drivers, drill rigs, and caisson drills. Impact and vibratory pile drivers (driven piles — steel H, precast concrete), drill rigs/auger equipment (drilled shafts, auger-cast piles), and caisson drills (large-diameter drilled piers). Specialized subcontractors and equipment. Production rates (piles per day, LF per day) are critical schedule drivers. Noise and vibration impact adjacent structures.
Industry Standard: ASTM D1143; ASTM D4945; IBC 1810
Common Values: Pile Driver — Impact, Pile Driver — Vibratory, Drill Rig / Auger, Caisson Drill
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; HCSS; PileNavigator; specialty geotechnical
Procore Coverage: Low — daily log equipment captures presence; pile driving logs are specialty data outside PM systems
Benchmarking Use: Compare pile driving production rate (piles/day) and deep foundation cost per LF by soil condition
Notes: Not present on every project — depends on soil conditions and structural loads. Pile driving logs and load test results are specialty data. Vibration monitoring may be required near existing structures.

Description: Transport equipment — dump trucks, flatbeds, and water trucks. Dump trucks (on/off-site material hauling for earthwork and demo), flatbed trucks (material delivery and transport), and water trucks (dust control, compaction moisture). Hauling volume drives earthwork cost. Cycle time (load, haul, dump, return) is the key production metric.
Industry Standard: DOT weight limits; OSHA 1926 Subpart O
Common Values: Dump Truck, Flatbed Truck, Water Truck, Articulated Hauler
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; HCSS HeavyJob; Fleet: GPS tracking
Procore Coverage: Low — daily log equipment may capture truck presence; no structured haul cycle data
Benchmarking Use: Compare haul cost per CY by distance and material type
Notes: Water trucks are critical for dust control (EPA/local regulations) and compaction moisture. Articulated haulers for soft ground conditions. Haul distance is the primary cost variable.

Description: Temporary site services during construction — power, compressed air, heating, lighting, and dewatering. Generators/temp power (before permanent electrical service), air compressors (pneumatic tools, testing, blowdown), heaters/temp heat (cold-weather concrete curing, winter enclosures), light towers (site lighting for early/late shifts), and dewatering pumps (groundwater control during excavation). Duration on site varies from mobilization to permanent service connection.
Industry Standard: OSHA 1926 Subpart K; NEC Article 590; NFPA 241
Common Values: Generator, Air Compressor, Heater / Temp Heat, Light Tower, Dewatering Pump
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; Rental companies; Utility providers
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily log equipment captures temporary utility presence; cost tracked under general conditions
Benchmarking Use: Compare temporary utility cost as % of general conditions by project duration and climate zone
Notes: Dewatering is critical on waterfront and high-water-table projects — can be a major unplanned cost. Temp heat is a significant winter cost in cold climates. Duration drives cost.

Description: On-site welding and cutting equipment — arc welders and cutting tools. Arc welders (SMAW, GMAW, FCAW machines for structural and miscellaneous welding) and cutting equipment (oxy-fuel, plasma, saw cutting for steel modification and demolition). Welding procedures require qualification per AWS. Field welding inspection is quality-critical.
Industry Standard: AWS D1.1; OSHA 1926 Subpart J
Common Values: Arc Welder (SMAW, GMAW, FCAW), Oxy-Fuel Torch, Plasma Cutter, Saw
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; Welding equipment suppliers
Procore Coverage: Low — daily log equipment may capture presence; welding inspection records are specialty data
Benchmarking Use: Compare field welding cost per connection and inspection rejection rate by joint type
Notes: Welding procedure specifications (WPS) and welder qualification records (WPQ) are required documentation but rarely tracked in PM systems. NDT inspection results are external.

Description: Surveying and layout instruments — total stations, GPS/GNSS, lasers, and drones. Total stations (electronic survey instrument for precise layout), GPS/GNSS equipment (satellite positioning for grading and layout — enables machine guidance), laser levels (rotary and line lasers for interior work), and drones/UAVs (aerial survey, progress photos, volumetric calculations, thermal scans). Layout accuracy is foundational — errors cascade to every trade.
Industry Standard: ASCE Manual of Practice 98; NSPS Standards; FAA Part 107 (drones)
Common Values: Total Station, GPS / GNSS Receiver, Robotic Total Station, Laser Level, Drone / UAV
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; Trimble; Leica; Topcon; DJI; Pix4D; DroneDeploy
Procore Coverage: Low — daily log equipment may note survey activities; no structured survey or drone data in PM systems
Benchmarking Use: Compare survey cost per acre and drone flight frequency per project by type and size
Notes: Drones increasingly common — progress photos, LiDAR, volumetric surveys, thermal scans. GPS machine guidance integration reduces survey staking. Survey data typically lives in specialty platforms.

Category: --- MATERIALS: permanent and temporary materials installed in a constructed facility. 25 categories organized by MasterFormat division — from concrete through conveying systems. ---

Description: All cast-in-place and precast concrete — the most common structural material in construction. Types: ready-mix concrete (normal weight, lightweight, high-strength, SCC, fiber-reinforced), precast structural (hollow core plank, double tees, beams, columns), precast architectural (panels, trim, ornamentation), concrete accessories (formwork, ties, chairs, embeds, anchors, waterstops), and admixtures/curing compounds. Mix design submittals are a key deliverable. Pour records tracked in daily logs.
Industry Standard: CSI MasterFormat 03 00 00; ACI 301; ACI 318; ASTM C94
Common Values: Ready-Mix, Lightweight, High-Strength, SCC, Fiber-Reinforced, Precast Structural, Precast Architectural, Admixtures
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; Concrete suppliers; BIM (Revit/Tekla); HCSS
Procore Coverage: Medium — submittals capture mix designs; daily log delivery and productivity capture pour records; cost codes under Division 03
Benchmarking Use: Compare concrete cost per CY and pour cycle time by structural system and project type
Notes: Mix design submittals are universal. Pour records in daily logs are a strong production signal. Precast requires shop drawing submittals. Accessories are consumed materials — rarely formally submitted.

Description: All concrete reinforcement — deformed bars, welded wire, post-tensioning, and mechanical couplers. Rebar (#3 through #18 deformed bars, epoxy coated), welded wire fabric (WWF/WWR for slabs), post-tensioning (PT strand, anchors, ducts, grout — specialty sub), and couplers/splices (mechanical rebar couplers, threaded inserts). Rebar shop drawings are standard submittals. Tonnage and placement rate drive schedule.
Industry Standard: CSI MasterFormat 03 20 00; ACI 318; CRSI Manual of Standard Practice
Common Values: Rebar #3–#18, Epoxy-Coated, WWF/WWR, PT Strand, Mechanical Couplers
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; Rebar fabricators; BIM (Revit/Tekla); ASA
Procore Coverage: Medium — submittals capture shop drawings; daily log delivery tracks rebar deliveries; cost codes under Division 03
Benchmarking Use: Compare rebar tonnage per CY of concrete by structural system and seismic zone
Notes: Rebar shop drawings are standard submittals on every concrete project. Post-tensioning is a separate specialty sub with its own submittals. Seismic zones drive rebar density.

Description: All structural and miscellaneous steel and metal fabrications. Structural steel (wide flange, HSS, angles, channels, plates), steel deck (composite, non-composite, and roof deck), connection hardware (bolts, welds, base plates, shear studs), miscellaneous metals (loose lintels, angles, embed plates), metal fabrications (stairs, railings, ladders, grating, bollards), and ornamental metals (decorative railings, screens, canopies). Shop drawing submittals with connection details are a major coordination effort.
Industry Standard: CSI MasterFormat 05 00 00; AISC 360; AWS D1.1; SDI
Common Values: Wide Flange, HSS, Angles, Composite Deck, Roof Deck, Misc Metals, Stairs, Railings, Bollards, Ornamental
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; Tekla; SDS/2; Fabsuite; BIM (Revit)
Procore Coverage: Medium — submittals capture shop drawings; daily log delivery tracks steel deliveries; cost codes under Division 05
Benchmarking Use: Compare structural steel cost per ton and erection rate by building type and height
Notes: Steel connection details generate heavy RFI activity — a leading indicator of structural complexity. Misc metals coordinate with nearly every other trade.

Description: All wood and composite products — dimensional lumber, engineered wood, and sheathing. Dimensional lumber (studs, joists, blocking, plates), engineered wood (LVL, glulam, TJI, CLT, PSL), and sheathing/panels (plywood, OSB, cementitious board). Engineered wood products require submittals. Mass timber (CLT, glulam) is a growing structural system. Less formal submittal requirements than steel or concrete for conventional framing.
Industry Standard: CSI MasterFormat 06 00 00 – 06 17 00; AWC NDS; APA
Common Values: Dimensional Lumber, LVL, Glulam, CLT, PSL, TJI, Plywood, OSB, Cementitious Board
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; BIM (Revit); Lumber suppliers
Procore Coverage: Medium — submittals required for engineered wood; daily log delivery tracks lumber deliveries; cost codes under Division 06
Benchmarking Use: Compare lumber cost per SF of floor area by framing type and building height
Notes: CLT and mass timber are a growing structural system with distinct submittal and fire protection requirements. Conventional framing has less formal submittals.

Description: All unit masonry — CMU, face brick, natural stone, manufactured stone, and accessories. CMU (standard, lightweight, split face, ground face), face brick (modular, queen, king, thin brick), natural stone (limestone, granite, marble, sandstone, slate), manufactured stone (cast stone, cultured stone, adhered veneer), mortar and grout (Type S, N, M), and accessories (ties, anchors, reinforcing, flashing, weeps, lintels). Color and pattern submittals are standard. Weather-sensitive installation.
Industry Standard: CSI MasterFormat 04 00 00; TMS 402/602; BIA Technical Notes
Common Values: CMU, Face Brick, Thin Brick, Limestone, Granite, Cast Stone, Cultured Stone, Mortar, Ties, Lintels
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; BIM (Revit); Masonry suppliers
Procore Coverage: Medium — submittals capture color/pattern selections; daily log delivery tracks masonry deliveries; cost codes under Division 04
Benchmarking Use: Compare masonry cost per SF of wall face by unit type and wall height
Notes: Color and pattern submittals are critical for approval. Masonry is seasonal in cold climates. Natural stone is often a separate specialty sub from brick/block.

Description: All roofing materials — membrane, metal, built-up, and accessories. Single-ply membrane (TPO, PVC, EPDM), built-up/modified bitumen (BUR, SBS, APP), metal roofing (standing seam, corrugated, architectural panels), insulation (polyiso, EPS, XPS, cover board), and accessories (fasteners, adhesive, walkway pads, snow guards, drains). Manufacturer warranty submittals are a key deliverable — warranty requirements drive material and installation specifications.
Industry Standard: CSI MasterFormat 07 50 00 – 07 70 00; NRCA Roofing Manual; FM Global; UL
Common Values: TPO, PVC, EPDM, BUR, Modified Bitumen, Standing Seam, Polyiso, EPS, XPS
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; Manufacturer reps; BIM (Revit)
Procore Coverage: Medium — submittals capture warranty specs; daily log delivery tracks roofing material deliveries; cost codes under Division 07
Benchmarking Use: Compare roofing cost per square by system type and climate zone
Notes: Manufacturer warranty requirements dictate material specs and installer certification. Weather-sensitive installation — daily log delays frequently reference roofing holds.

Description: All moisture protection — below-grade waterproofing, air/vapor barriers, sealants, flashing, and expansion joints. Below-grade waterproofing (sheet membrane, fluid-applied, bentonite, drainage board), air/vapor barriers (fluid-applied, self-adhered, mechanically fastened), sealants and caulking (silicone, urethane, hybrid, backer rod), flashing and sheet metal (through-wall, counter, cap, reglet, copings), and expansion joints. Failure leads to long-term moisture intrusion — a top rework driver.
Industry Standard: CSI MasterFormat 07 10 00 – 07 95 00; ASTM E2112; ABAA
Common Values: Sheet Membrane, Fluid-Applied, Bentonite, Air Barrier, Silicone Sealant, Urethane, Flashing, Expansion Joint
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; Bluebeam; Manufacturer reps
Procore Coverage: Medium — submittals capture product specs; quality observations capture waterproofing failures; cost codes under Division 07
Benchmarking Use: Compare waterproofing defect rate and warranty claim frequency by membrane type and climate
Notes: Waterproofing failures are consistently among the top rework drivers. Air barrier is increasingly a code requirement (IECC). Observation data is a strong signal for envelope failures.

Description: Insulation and fireproofing materials — building insulation, spray fireproofing, and firestopping. Building insulation (batt, rigid board, spray foam, blown-in — energy code driven), spray fireproofing (SFRM — cementitious and intumescent on structural steel), and firestopping (through-penetration, perimeter, and joint firestopping). Spray fireproofing follows steel erection and is critical path. Firestopping is inspection-critical — one of the most common inspection findings.
Industry Standard: CSI MasterFormat 07 20 00 – 07 84 00; IECC; ASHRAE 90.1; UL Firestop Systems; IBC Chapter 7
Common Values: Batt Insulation, Rigid Board, Spray Foam, SFRM (Cementitious), SFRM (Intumescent), Firestopping
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; UL Product iQ; Manufacturer reps
Procore Coverage: Medium — inspections capture firestopping compliance; submittals track product specs; cost codes under Division 07
Benchmarking Use: Compare firestopping deficiency rate per floor and insulation cost per SF by climate zone
Notes: Firestopping deficiencies are among the most common inspection findings across all project types. Spray fireproofing thickness is a frequent inspection checkpoint.

Description: All door assemblies — hollow metal, wood, specialty doors, finish hardware, and overhead doors. Hollow metal doors and frames (steel), wood doors (flush, stile and rail, fire-rated), specialty doors (FRP, aluminum, sliding, folding, acoustic), finish hardware (hinges, locksets, closers, exit devices, stops, ADA compliant), and overhead/coiling doors (roll-up, sectional, fire-rated coiling). The hardware schedule is one of the most detailed submittals on any project.
Industry Standard: CSI MasterFormat 08 10 00 – 08 71 00; DHI; BHMA
Common Values: Hollow Metal Door, Wood Door, Specialty Door, Hinges, Locksets, Closers, Exit Devices, Overhead Door
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; BIM (Revit door schedule); Manufacturer reps
Procore Coverage: Medium — submittals capture hardware schedules (high volume); daily log delivery tracks door deliveries; cost codes under Division 08
Benchmarking Use: Compare door assembly cost per opening and hardware submittal cycle time by project type
Notes: Hardware schedule is one of the most detailed and time-consuming submittals. ADA compliance drives hardware selection. Fire-rated assemblies require labeled components.

Description: All glass and glazing systems — curtain wall, windows, storefronts, skylights, and insulating glass units. Curtain wall systems (unitized and stick-built aluminum/glass facades), windows (aluminum, vinyl, wood — operable and fixed), storefronts and entrances (aluminum storefront, entrance doors, ADA), skylights (unit, ridge, pyramid, smoke vents), and IGUs (tempered, laminated, low-E, tinted, spandrel). Performance specs (U-factor, SHGC, VLT) drive selection. Major shop drawing submittals.
Industry Standard: CSI MasterFormat 08 40 00 – 08 81 00; AAMA; ASTM E2190; NFRC
Common Values: Unitized Curtain Wall, Stick-Built CW, Aluminum Window, Storefront, Skylight, IGU (Low-E, Tempered, Laminated)
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; BIM (Revit); Fabricator shop drawing systems
Procore Coverage: Medium — submittals capture shop drawings and performance specs; daily log delivery tracks glazing deliveries; cost codes under Division 08
Benchmarking Use: Compare curtain wall cost per SF of facade and submittal turnaround time by system type
Notes: Curtain wall is typically long lead — fabrication submittals are early and critical. Performance specs (thermal, structural, water) drive glazing selection. Unitized vs stick-built is a major design/schedule decision.

Description: Metal framing and gypsum board — the backbone of interior partitions. Metal studs and track (light-gauge steel framing, deflection track, furring), gypsum board (regular, moisture-resistant, fire-rated, abuse-resistant, shaftliner), and plaster/stucco (traditional plaster, EIFS, synthetic stucco). Specialty boards (moisture, abuse, fire, shaftliner) require submittals. Gypsum board quantities are among the largest material volumes on any interior project.
Industry Standard: CSI MasterFormat 09 20 00 – 09 29 00; ASTM C840; GA-216; ASTM C1396
Common Values: Metal Studs, Deflection Track, Regular Gypsum, Moisture-Resistant, Fire-Rated, Abuse-Resistant, Shaftliner, EIFS
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; BIM (Revit); Manufacturer reps
Procore Coverage: Medium — submittals required for specialty boards; cost codes under Division 09
Benchmarking Use: Compare gypsum board cost per SF of partition and framing labor productivity by partition type
Notes: Specialty board requirements drive cost — abuse-resistant and fire-rated boards cost significantly more than standard. EIFS crosses into exterior work.

Description: All ceiling systems — acoustic grid-and-tile and specialty ceilings. Acoustic ceilings (suspension grid, lay-in tile, tegular) and specialty ceilings (wood, metal, stretch, linear, open cell, cloud ceilings). Acoustic tile selection affects NRC ratings and aesthetics. Specialty ceilings are increasingly common in commercial lobbies and open office areas. Ceiling installation cannot begin until above-ceiling MEP is inspected and approved.
Industry Standard: CSI MasterFormat 09 50 00 – 09 54 00; ASTM E1264; CISCA
Common Values: Grid, Lay-In Tile, Tegular, Wood Ceiling, Metal Ceiling, Stretch Ceiling, Linear, Cloud
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; BIM (Revit ceiling plans); Manufacturer reps
Procore Coverage: Medium — submittals capture product specs; cost codes under Division 09
Benchmarking Use: Compare ceiling cost per SF by system type and NRC requirement
Notes: Ceiling grid installation is a coordination milestone — triggers MEP above-ceiling inspection. Specialty ceilings are significantly more expensive than standard acoustic tile.

Description: All flooring materials — tile, carpet, resilient, hardwood, stone, and resinous/specialty. Ceramic/porcelain tile (floor and wall tile, mosaics), carpet (broadloom, modular tile), resilient flooring (LVT, VCT, sheet vinyl, rubber, linoleum), hardwood (solid, engineered, prefinished), stone (marble, granite, terrazzo, slate), and resinous/specialty (epoxy, urethane, MMA, polished concrete, stained). Substrate moisture content must meet ASTM F2170 before resilient and wood installation.
Industry Standard: CSI MasterFormat 09 30 00 – 09 68 00; TCNA Handbook; CRI-104; NWFA; ASTM F2170
Common Values: Ceramic Tile, Porcelain, Carpet Tile, LVT, VCT, Rubber, Hardwood, Terrazzo, Epoxy, Polished Concrete
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; BIM (Revit floor finish plan); Manufacturer reps
Procore Coverage: Medium — submittals capture product specs and color selections; punch items reference flooring defects; cost codes under Division 09
Benchmarking Use: Compare flooring cost per SF by material type and defect rate per installer
Notes: Substrate moisture testing is a common delay driver — concrete moisture content must meet ASTM F2170. Multiple flooring subs per project is typical. Punch items frequently reference flooring.

Description: All painting and coating products — interior paint, exterior coatings, specialty coatings, primers, and wall coverings. Interior paint (latex, acrylic for walls, ceilings, trim), exterior paint and coatings (elastomeric, acrylic, high-performance), specialty coatings (epoxy, urethane, anti-graffiti, antimicrobial), primers and sealers (metal primer, wood sealer, block filler), and wall coverings (vinyl, fabric, FRP panels). Paint schedules are a standard submittal.
Industry Standard: CSI MasterFormat 09 72 00 – 09 97 00; MPI Standards; SSPC
Common Values: Latex, Acrylic, Elastomeric, Epoxy, Urethane, Anti-Graffiti, Vinyl Wall Covering, FRP
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; Manufacturer reps
Procore Coverage: Medium — submittals capture paint schedule and color selections; punch items reference paint touch-ups at high frequency
Benchmarking Use: Compare paint cost per SF and touch-up punch item rate by project type
Notes: Paint/finish is consistently the #1 punch item category across all project types. Color selection submittals are time-consuming. VOC compliance drives product selection.

Description: Division 10 specialties — toilet accessories, signage, fire protection specialties, wall/corner protection, and lockers/storage. Toilet accessories (partitions, grab bars, dispensers, mirrors), signage (interior/exterior, ADA, wayfinding), fire protection specialties (extinguishers, cabinets, blankets), wall and corner protection (corner guards, wall protection, handrails), and lockers/storage (lockers, shelving, mail boxes). Small individual scopes but numerous SKUs across a project.
Industry Standard: CSI MasterFormat 10 00 00; ADA Standards for Accessible Design; NFPA 10
Common Values: Toilet Partitions, Grab Bars, Dispensers, Mirrors, ADA Signs, Wayfinding, Extinguisher Cabinets, Corner Guards, Lockers
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; BIM (Revit); Manufacturer reps
Procore Coverage: Medium — submittals capture product specs; cost codes under Division 10
Benchmarking Use: Compare specialty cost per SF by building occupancy type
Notes: Often supplied by GC directly or by multiple specialty suppliers. ADA compliance drives signage and accessory selection. Fire protection specialties are code-required.

Description: Cabinets, countertops, trim, and custom millwork. Plastic laminate casework (standard commercial cabinets), wood casework (stain-grade cabinets), countertops (solid surface, quartz, granite, laminate, stainless), and trim/millwork (base, crown, casing, chair rail, paneling). Shop drawing submittals are standard — fabrication lead time drives schedule. Quality is highly visible to end users.
Industry Standard: CSI MasterFormat 06 22 00 – 06 60 00; AWI Quality Standards (Economy/Custom/Premium)
Common Values: Plastic Laminate, Wood Casework, Quartz Counter, Granite Counter, Solid Surface, Stainless, Base/Crown Trim
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; BIM (Revit casework model); Custom fabricators
Procore Coverage: Medium — submittals capture shop drawings; daily log delivery tracks casework deliveries; cost codes under Division 06
Benchmarking Use: Compare casework cost per LF and shop drawing cycle time by quality grade (Economy/Custom/Premium)
Notes: AWI quality grades (Economy, Custom, Premium) drive cost and specification detail. Long fabrication lead time — shop drawings are early critical path. Highly visible to end users — punch item driver.

Description: All plumbing piping, fittings, and fixtures. Pipe: domestic water (copper, PEX, CPVC, stainless), waste/vent (cast iron, PVC, ABS, no-hub), valves and fittings (gate, ball, check, PRV, backflow preventers), and hangers/supports. Fixtures: water closets and urinals, lavatories and sinks, faucets and trim, carriers and supports, water heaters (tank, tankless, heat pump), and specialty fixtures (drinking fountains, eyewash, emergency showers). Every fixture requires a model-specific submittal.
Industry Standard: CSI MasterFormat 22 00 00; IPC; UPC; ASME B31; ASSE
Common Values: Copper, PEX, CPVC, Cast Iron, PVC, Flush Valve, Tank Type, Wall-Hung, Undermount, Tankless Water Heater
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; BIM (Revit plumbing model); Manufacturer reps
Procore Coverage: Medium — submittals capture every fixture by model (high volume); daily log delivery tracks fixture deliveries; cost codes under Division 22
Benchmarking Use: Compare plumbing cost per fixture and submittal turnaround time by building type
Notes: Plumbing fixture submittals are high-volume — every fixture by model number. Fixture count is a primary plumbing cost driver. Backflow preventer submittals are code-critical.

Description: All HVAC ductwork, piping, and insulation. Ductwork: sheet metal duct (rectangular, round, oval), flexible duct, duct fittings and accessories (elbows, tees, dampers, turning vanes, access doors), and diffusers/grilles/registers (supply, return, exhaust terminals). Piping: hydronic piping (chilled/hot water, condenser water), refrigerant piping (copper lines, fittings), and mechanical insulation (fiberglass, elastomeric, mineral wool, jacketing). Largest volume of material in above-ceiling spaces.
Industry Standard: CSI MasterFormat 23 07 00 – 23 37 00; SMACNA Standards; ASHRAE; ASTM
Common Values: Rectangular Duct, Round Duct, Flex Duct, Dampers, Diffusers, CHW Pipe, Refrigerant Pipe, Pipe Insulation
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; BIM (Revit MEP model); Sheet metal fabricators
Procore Coverage: Medium — submittals capture duct and terminal device specs; cost codes under Division 23
Benchmarking Use: Compare HVAC duct cost per SF of conditioned area and distribution labor hours by system type
Notes: Duct fabrication is increasingly off-site (shop fab vs field fab). Above-ceiling coordination with other MEP trades is a major scheduling challenge. Terminal device submittals are high-volume.

Description: Major HVAC equipment and building automation controls. Equipment: air handling units (central station AHUs, make-up air), rooftop units (packaged RTUs, DX cooling), split systems/VRF/heat pumps, chillers (air-cooled, water-cooled), cooling towers, boilers/furnaces, HVAC pumps (chilled water, hot water, condenser), and terminal units (VAV boxes, fan coils, unit heaters). Controls: thermostats/sensors, actuators/valves, DDC controllers, BAS panels. Major equipment submittals are early critical path.
Industry Standard: CSI MasterFormat 23 09 00 – 23 81 00; ASHRAE Standards; AHRI; UL
Common Values: AHU, RTU, VRF, Chiller, Cooling Tower, Boiler, VAV Box, Fan Coil, BAS Panel, DDC Controller
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; BIM (Revit equipment schedules); Tridium (controls); Manufacturer reps
Procore Coverage: Medium — submittals capture major equipment (long lead); daily log delivery tracks equipment arrivals; cost codes under Division 23
Benchmarking Use: Compare HVAC equipment cost per ton of cooling and commissioning deficiency rate by system type
Notes: Major HVAC equipment is long lead — submittal approval drives procurement schedule. Controls/BAS commissioning is a late-project critical path. VRF is growing market share vs traditional chilled water.

Description: Power distribution equipment and wiring systems. Distribution: switchgear, transformers (dry-type, pad-mount), panelboards and breakers, disconnects and switches, motor starters/VFDs, and generators/UPS (permanent standby power). Wiring: wire and cable (THHN, MC, armored, fire alarm, medium voltage), conduit (EMT, rigid, IMC, PVC, flexible), cable tray and wireways, boxes and fittings, and devices (receptacles, switches, plates, floor boxes). Switchgear submittal is an early critical path item.
Industry Standard: CSI MasterFormat 26 05 00 – 26 32 00; NEC (NFPA 70); IEEE; UL
Common Values: Switchgear, Transformer, Panel, VFD, Generator, UPS, THHN Wire, MC Cable, EMT, Rigid Conduit, Cable Tray
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; BIM (Revit electrical model); Manufacturer reps
Procore Coverage: Medium — submittals capture major equipment; daily log delivery tracks electrical deliveries; cost codes under Division 26
Benchmarking Use: Compare electrical cost per SF by building occupancy and electrical density (watts/SF)
Notes: Switchgear and transformer are long-lead items — submittal approval is early critical path. Generator sizing drives significant cost on mission-critical facilities (hospitals, data centers). VFDs increasingly standard.

Description: All light fixtures and controls — interior, exterior, emergency, and controls. Interior fixtures (troffers, downlights, pendants, linear, decorative), exterior fixtures (site, facade, parking, landscape lighting), emergency and exit (emergency battery units, exit signs, egress lighting — code-required), and lighting controls (dimmers, occupancy sensors, daylight harvesting, relay panels). Lighting submittals are among the highest volume on any project — every fixture type requires approval.
Industry Standard: CSI MasterFormat 26 50 00 – 26 56 00; NEC; IES; IECC; ASHRAE 90.1
Common Values: Troffer, Downlight, Pendant, Linear, Decorative, Site Light, Emergency Battery, Exit Sign, Occupancy Sensor, Dimmer
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; BIM (Revit lighting plan); AGi32; Manufacturer reps
Procore Coverage: Medium — submittals capture every fixture type (highest volume submittal); cost codes under Division 26
Benchmarking Use: Compare lighting fixture cost per SF and energy code compliance approach by climate zone
Notes: Lighting submittals are consistently the highest-volume submittal category. Energy code (IECC, ASHRAE 90.1) drives control requirements. LED is now standard — fixture cost has decreased while control complexity has increased.

Description: All fire suppression and fire alarm materials — sprinkler pipe, heads, pumps, detection devices, notification devices, and control panels. Suppression: sprinkler pipe and fittings (Schedule 10/40, CPVC, grooved, threaded), sprinkler heads (pendant, upright, sidewall, concealed), fire pump and controller, standpipe and FDC, and specialty systems (clean agent, kitchen hood, foam, pre-action). Alarm: detection devices (smoke, heat, duct, beam detectors), notification devices (horns, strobes, speakers), pull stations and modules, and FACP/network panels.
Industry Standard: CSI MasterFormat 21 00 00 / 28 31 00; NFPA 13; NFPA 72; FM Global; UL
Common Values: Wet Pipe, Dry Pipe, Pre-Action, Clean Agent, CPVC Pipe, Concealed Head, FACP, Smoke Detector, Strobe, Pull Station
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; BIM (Revit sprinkler/FA model); Manufacturer reps
Procore Coverage: Medium — submittals capture system specs; inspections capture hydrostatic test and acceptance test results; cost codes under Divisions 21 and 28
Benchmarking Use: Compare fire protection cost per SF by occupancy type and system type (wet vs dry vs specialty)
Notes: Fire suppression and fire alarm are increasingly separate subcontractors (separate unions and licensing). NFPA 13 compliance is inspection-critical. Fire alarm programming and acceptance testing is late-project critical path.

Description: Data/telecom, security, AV, and paging systems. Communications: structured cabling (Cat6, Cat6A, fiber optic), data outlets and patch panels, data racks and enclosures, AV equipment (displays, projectors, speakers, conferencing, control), and paging/intercom. Security: CCTV/cameras (IP cameras, NVR, video management), access control (card readers, controllers, electric strikes/maglocks), and intrusion detection (motion sensors, door contacts, alarm panels). Technology density varies widely by building type.
Industry Standard: CSI MasterFormat 27 00 00 – 28 00 00; BICSI; TIA/EIA-568; NFPA 72
Common Values: Cat6, Cat6A, Fiber Optic, Patch Panel, IP Camera, NVR, Card Reader, Maglocks, AV Display, Projector, Paging
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; BIM (Revit); IT integrators; Security integrators
Procore Coverage: Medium — submittals capture system specs; cost codes under Divisions 27-28
Benchmarking Use: Compare low voltage cost per SF by building type and technology density
Notes: Communications and security are often separate specialty subs. AV complexity has increased significantly — conference room technology is a major scope item. Access control drives hardware requirements on doors.

Description: Sitework and infrastructure materials — soil/fill, utility pipe, manholes, geotextiles, paving, fencing, and landscaping. Soil/fill material (structural fill, gravel, sand, aggregate, topsoil), utility pipe (ductile iron, PVC, HDPE, RCP, corrugated), manholes and structures (precast manholes, catch basins, junction boxes), geotextiles and erosion control (silt fence, wattles, stabilization fabric, geogrid), paving (asphalt, concrete paving, pavers), fencing and gates, and landscaping (plants, trees, irrigation, soil amendments).
Industry Standard: CSI MasterFormat 31 00 00 – 33 00 00; AASHTO; ASTM D698; EPA SWPPP
Common Values: Structural Fill, Gravel, DI Pipe, PVC Pipe, HDPE, Precast Manhole, Silt Fence, Asphalt, Concrete Paving, Chain Link
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; HCSS HeavyJob; Paving contractors; Utility subs
Procore Coverage: Medium — submittals required for utility pipe; daily log delivery tracks material arrivals; daily log delay captures weather/site issues
Benchmarking Use: Compare site work cost per acre and utility cost per LF of trench by soil condition
Notes: Soil/fill material is not typically in PM material tables — tracked via delivery logs and procurement. Paving and landscaping are late-project scopes. Geotechnical testing drives compaction specifications.

Description: All vertical transportation — traction elevators, hydraulic elevators, MRL (machine-room-less) elevators, escalators, moving walks, and cab finishes. Traction elevators (gearless and geared — high-rise standard), hydraulic elevators (low-rise, freight), MRL elevators (growing mid-rise market), escalators and moving walks, and cab finishes (interior panels, ceilings, flooring, handrails). Typically proprietary — one manufacturer's installer per project. Long equipment lead time.
Industry Standard: CSI MasterFormat 14 00 00; ASME A17.1; ANSI A17.1
Common Values: Traction Elevator, Hydraulic Elevator, MRL Elevator, Escalator, Moving Walk, Cab Finishes
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; Elevator OEMs (Otis, Schindler, KONE, thyssenkrupp)
Procore Coverage: Medium — submittals capture elevator specs; vendor directory identifies elevator sub; cost codes under Division 14
Benchmarking Use: Compare elevator cost per stop and installation duration per floor by system type
Notes: Proprietary trade — limited submittal detail due to manufacturer IP. Elevator inspection is jurisdictional. MRL systems are gaining market share over traditional traction in mid-rise buildings.

Category: --- STANDARD MEASURES: KPIs and metrics derived from resource entities — labor, equipment, and materials. ---

Description: Output per labor hour for a given trade — the fundamental measure of workforce efficiency. Calculation: units installed ÷ total labor hours. Units vary by trade (CY for concrete, SF for drywall, tons for steel). Requires daily log manpower hours and daily log productivity/quantity records. Source entities: manpower logs, productivity logs, timecard entries.
Industry Standard: AACE RP 22R-01; CII Benchmarking
Common Values: 0.5–2.0 SF/labor-hour (drywall), 0.3–1.0 CY/labor-hour (concrete), varies widely by trade
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; HCSS; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily log manpower captures hours; daily log productivity captures quantities; linking requires matching trade to output
Benchmarking Use: Compare labor productivity rates across project types and geographic regions for the same trade
Notes: Requires clean trade-to-output mapping — often not available in structured data. Weather, overtime, and learning curve all affect rates. Best measured at trade level, not aggregate.

Description: Average and peak headcount for a specific trade on a project. Calculation: sum of workers reported per trade per day from manpower logs. Tracking over time shows workforce loading curve — early ramp, peak, and demobilization. Source entities: manpower logs.
Industry Standard: OSHA recordkeeping; CII Workforce Planning
Common Values: 5–15 (typical crew), 50–200+ (peak on large projects)
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; HCSS; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily log manpower captures headcount by trade; workforce timecard tables exist but not widely available
Benchmarking Use: Compare peak crew size and workforce loading shape by project type and total value band
Notes: Trade name normalization is essential — same trade may be logged under different names across companies. Headcount-based, not FTE-based.

Description: Cost of labor per unit of output — combines productivity and wage rate. Calculation: total labor cost for trade ÷ total units installed. Requires cost code breakdown by trade and quantity tracking. Source entities: cost codes, manpower logs, productivity logs, financial commitments.
Industry Standard: AACE Cost Engineering Terminology
Common Values: $5–$15/SF (drywall), $50–$150/CY (concrete placement), varies by market
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Medium — cost codes capture labor cost; linking to units requires productivity data or budget line item units
Benchmarking Use: Compare labor cost per unit by trade across regions and project types to identify efficiency outliers
Notes: Wage rates vary significantly by region and union status. Overtime premium distorts per-unit cost. Best used for relative comparison, not absolute targets.

Description: Percentage of available time that equipment is actively in use. Calculation: active operating hours ÷ total available hours on site. High utilization indicates right-sizing; low utilization suggests oversized fleet or scheduling gaps. Source entities: equipment logs, telematics.
Industry Standard: CII Equipment Management
Common Values: 60–85% (target utilization)
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Fleet: telematics platforms; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Low — daily log equipment captures presence but not operating hours; telematics data lives outside PM systems
Benchmarking Use: Compare equipment utilization rates by equipment type and project phase to optimize fleet sizing
Notes: Telematics provide the best utilization data but are rarely integrated into PM systems. Daily log equipment captures on-site presence, not active use.

Description: Percentage of material ordered that is not installed — waste, damage, theft, or over-ordering. Calculation: (quantity ordered − quantity installed) ÷ quantity ordered × 100. Typical rates: 2–5% for steel, 5–10% for concrete, 10–15% for drywall. Source entities: procurement records, productivity logs, delivery logs.
Industry Standard: WRAP (Waste & Resources Action Programme); LEED MR credits
Common Values: 2–5% (steel), 5–10% (concrete), 10–15% (drywall/lumber), varies by material
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; BIM (takeoff vs installed)
Procore Coverage: Low — delivery logs capture inbound quantities; productivity logs capture installed quantities; but linking the two is manual
Benchmarking Use: Compare material waste rates by material type and project type to identify over-ordering patterns
Notes: Waste tracking is increasingly important for LEED credits and ESG reporting. BIM-to-field quantity comparison is the emerging approach. Most companies track waste informally.

Description: Days from submittal submission to final approval — a leading indicator of schedule risk. Calculation: approval_date − submission_date for each submittal. Long turnaround on critical submittals (structural steel, curtain wall, major equipment) delays procurement and fabrication. Source entities: submittals.
Industry Standard: AIA A201 (standard review periods)
Common Values: 7–14 days (typical target), 21–45 days (complex submittals)
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; Bluebeam
Procore Coverage: High — submittals table tracks submission and approval dates with structured status workflow
Benchmarking Use: Compare submittal turnaround time by division and reviewer to identify bottlenecks
Notes: Long turnaround on critical-path submittals (steel shop drawings, curtain wall, major MEP equipment) is a leading schedule risk indicator. Re-submittals compound the delay.

Description: Number of distinct trade categories active on a project — a proxy for project complexity. Calculation: count of unique trades from manpower logs. More trades = more coordination overhead. Typical: 15–25 trades on a mid-size commercial project. Source entities: manpower logs, vendor directory.
Industry Standard: CII Complexity Assessment
Common Values: 8–12 (simple), 15–25 (typical commercial), 25–35 (complex healthcare/lab)
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily log manpower captures trade names; vendor directory captures subcontractor list
Benchmarking Use: Compare trade count by project type and total value to benchmark coordination complexity
Notes: Trade count is a useful complexity proxy but requires normalized trade names. More trades = more potential conflicts and coordination meetings.

Description: Percentage of material deliveries arriving on or before the scheduled date. Calculation: on-time deliveries ÷ total scheduled deliveries × 100. Late deliveries cause trade stacking and schedule compression. Source entities: delivery logs, procurement schedules.
Industry Standard: CII Materials Management
Common Values: 85–95% (target), varies by material and supply chain conditions
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel; Procurement platforms
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily log delivery captures actual arrival; submittal required-on-site dates provide the scheduled baseline
Benchmarking Use: Compare on-time delivery rate by material category and supplier to identify supply chain risk
Notes: Submittal overdue flags and required-on-site dates are the closest proxy for delivery schedule compliance. Actual delivery date tracking is sparse in most systems.

Description: Total labor hours normalized by project value — an efficiency metric that enables cross-project comparison regardless of size. Calculation: total manpower hours ÷ (project total value ÷ $1M). Higher values indicate labor-intensive projects (renovation, healthcare). Source entities: manpower logs, project attributes.
Industry Standard: CII Benchmarking; ENR Cost Data
Common Values: 2000–5000 hours/$1M (new construction), 4000–8000 hours/$1M (renovation)
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; MS Excel
Procore Coverage: Medium — daily log manpower captures hours; project total value from project attributes
Benchmarking Use: Compare labor intensity across project types and delivery methods
Notes: Renovation projects are significantly more labor-intensive per dollar than new construction. Normalizing by value enables comparison across project sizes.
